summary: The meeting was an initial project kickoff for developing a novel remote control. The main purpose was to introduce team members, establish project goals, and discuss early design concepts and user requirements.

Project Manager: Emphasized the need for an original, user-friendly remote control suitable for all ages
Highlighted financial constraints with a €25 selling price and €12.50 production cost

Expressed concern about complexity for older users
Proposed a flip-top design for a larger screen
Marketing: Advocated for simplification of symbols and user interface
Suggested incorporating an LCD display with menus similar to mobile phones
Proposed a large print option for older generations
Emphasized independence and free-spiritedness as key design qualities
Questioned whether plastic would be cheaper and maintain fashion appeal
User Interface: Supported an LCD display with menu navigation similar to mobile phones
Emphasized the need for intuitive navigation
Suggested reducing physical buttons to prevent complexity
Agreed with dual-function design approach
Noted that simplified symbols would improve usability
Industrial Designer: Expressed concern about cost implications of advanced features like LCD screens
Suggested self-intuitive design with obvious pictures and symbols
Noted that plastic could still achieve a fashionable appearance
Mentioned that a black and white LCD might be more feasible
Emphasized the need for the design to be self-intuitive for all users

summary: The meeting was an evidence session for the Children, Young People and Education Committee regarding the Children (Abolition of Defence of Reasonable Punishment) (Wales) Bill. The Crown Prosecution Service (CPS) officials provided insights on the legal framework, implications of removing the reasonable chastisement defence, and practical implementation challenges.

Barry Hughes: The current law provides fewer rights to children than adults by allowing the reasonable chastisement defence for assaults on children.
Removing the defence would make the law clearer but create grey areas in prosecution decisions.
The CPS anticipates very low prosecution numbers (low single figures) due to the public interest test.
The law should evolve to reflect contemporary societal standards, as seen in changes like marital rape laws.
Cross-border issues with England will require awareness campaigns but are manageable.
Sian Gwenllian AM: Questioned whether the 2007 government review adequately addressed changing societal views on punishment.
Expressed concern about potential for more prosecutions at the 'softer end' of the spectrum.
Raised the issue of unintended consequences, including criminal records for parents in Wales.
Asked about the risk of malicious reporting in private family law cases.
Questioned whether Wales could face ECHR compliance issues if the law changes.
Dawn Bowden AM: Raised concerns about legislative competence and the need for CPS guidance updates.
Questioned the necessity of a memorandum of understanding for cross-border issues.
Asked about CPS staff awareness of legal divergence between England and Wales.
Inquired about other significant cross-border issues beyond the defence removal.
Expressed satisfaction with the level of CPS involvement in developing the legislation.
Suzy Davies AM: Questioned how the burden of proof would work if the defence is removed.
Asked about preventing cases from reaching arrest stage to avoid DBS implications.
Expressed concern about the public interest test being critical but not yet finalized.
Questioned whether a civil law approach might achieve cultural change better.
Raised the issue of a child 'consenting' to punishment as a potential legal grey area.
Vikki Howells AM: Asked about factors in the public interest test, particularly regarding child impact.
Questioned the development of out-of-court disposals specific to this Bill.
Raised concerns about infrastructure for implementing disposals given legislative limits.
Questioned whether awareness-raising should be a duty in the Bill.
Asked about CPS approach to unintentional lawbreaking by visitors from abroad.

agreements.details: All agreed that prosecutions would be very low (low single figures) due to the public interest test.
Agreed that public awareness is crucial to inform people about the law change, especially cross-border.
Agreed that the CPS can adapt guidance and procedures to implement the law effectively.
Agreed that removing the defence affords children greater protection, aligning with their rights at age 16.
Agreed that the test will focus on proportionality, circumstances, and impact on the child.
Agreed that legal divergence with England is manageable but requires careful handling.
Agreed that CPS can be ready by implementation date (around 2022) with proper preparation.
Agreed that manual logging of cases will be feasible due to low numbers.

disagreements.details: Suzy Davies questioned if criminal law is the right approach for cultural change, suggesting civil law might be better.
Suzy Davies wanted to see draft CPS guidance before committing to the Bill, while Barry Hughes disagreed.
Disagreement on whether a child can 'consent' to a smack, with Barry Hughes noting legal complexity.
Sian Gwenllian expressed concern about increased malicious reports, while Barry Hughes downplayed the risk.
Sian Gwenllian questioned if Wales could face ECHR issues, while Barry Hughes noted current government position is compliant.
Sian Gwenllian worried about performance targets driving inappropriate prosecutions, while Barry Hughes dismissed this.
Vikki Howells questioned if awareness-raising should be a duty in the Bill, while Barry Hughes avoided taking a stance.
Initial confusion about burden of proof after removing the defence, clarified by Barry Hughes.

most_time_consuming.topic: Public interest test and prosecution thresholds
most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion extensively covered how the CPS would apply the public interest test, what factors would influence decisions, and how removing the defence would affect evidential thresholds. This included examples of grey areas, repeated offenses, and the balance between protecting children and avoiding unnecessary criminalization.

When and how to make meeting data publicly available

most_time_consuming.topic: Transcript approval process and legal concerns
most_time_consuming.reason: Extended discussion about transcript accuracy requirements, approval methods, and legal implications with no clear resolution

summary: The Children, Young People and Education Committee held an evidence session on education otherwise than at school (EOTAS), focusing on reasons for increased exclusions, support strategies, funding challenges, curriculum access, and reintegration efforts. The main purpose was to gather insights from local authority representatives on improving provision for vulnerable learners.

Nick Williams: Increased exclusions are driven by challenging behavior and inadequate capacity to address additional learning needs.
Local authorities are developing tiered behavior strategies but face system lag in implementation.
A team-around-the-family approach is essential for engaging parents of EOTAS learners.
Funding for PRU staff training is often an afterthought compared to school funding.
Transition support must continue after learners return to mainstream education.
Sharon Davies: Exclusion pressures stem from school accountability measures and funding constraints.
Torfaen works closely with schools on exclusions but sees improvement as early under new interim measures.
Multi-agency collaboration with social care and health is crucial for holistic support.
Funding variations make it difficult to plan for EOTAS provision.
Reintegration challenges increase at key stage 4 due to learner age and complexity.
David Hopkins: Narrow attainment measures have pressured schools to exclude vulnerable learners.
The new ALN legislation will strain resources across all sectors, including post-16.
Local authorities should adopt broader accountability measures to reduce exclusion incentives.
Commissioning frameworks for EOTAS providers require careful balancing of safeguarding and accessibility.
Inconsistencies exist in curriculum delivery for EOTAS learners, particularly in non-authority provision.
Dr Ian Johnson: Responsibility for EOTAS support lies with local authorities but requires coordination across institutions.
The gap between exclusion and entering EOTAS provision leaves vulnerable children at risk.
A whole-school approach should be statutory to prioritize well-being and mental health.
Teacher training must embed awareness of inequality and adverse childhood experiences.
EOTAS learners require prioritized well-being support within the new curriculum.
Liz Williams: Parents need better information to support children in EOTAS settings.
Teacher training should embed mental health awareness to reduce exclusion risks.
A whole-school ethos focusing on well-being is key to preventing disengagement.
Community recognition of ACE links to exclusion is vital for systemic change.

agreements.details: Challenging behavior and inadequate ALN provision capacity are primary exclusion causes.
Team-around-the-family approaches involving health, social care, and schools are essential.
ALN legislation and narrow accountability measures strain school resources.
EOTAS learners need broad, balanced curriculum aligned with mainstream standards.
Support must continue after learners return to mainstream education.
Teachers need specialized training on ACE, trauma, and mental health.
Quality assurance is critical for EOTAS provision, particularly in home settings.
Broader metrics are needed to reduce exclusion incentives.

disagreements.details: David Hopkins argues delegation levels don't directly affect exclusions, while Nick Williams cites funding lags in PRUs.
Nick and Sharon see improving consistency, while Dawn Bowden highlights discrepancies even in authority-provision.
Sharon Davies notes local authority restrictions on home visits, while Nick Williams uses direct staff for home tuition.
Nick Williams emphasizes safeguarding balance, while David Hopkins stresses third-party monitoring challenges.
David Hopkins believes needs should be met in all settings, while Dawn Bowden cites disconnects in practice.
Sharon Davies notes niche expertise gaps, while Nick Williams advocates for teacher training integration.
Nick supports devolved funding with action plans, while Sharon prefers multi-agency family approaches.
Dr Ian Johnson questions national implementation, while Nick notes regional collaboration.

most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion extensively covered funding pressures, ALN legislation impacts, delegation models, and capacity constraints, with multiple participants debating systemic challenges and potential solutions.

summary: The meeting focused on conceptualizing a new remote control design, emphasizing aesthetics, functionality, and technological innovation. Key discussions included material choices, button layout, color schemes, and potential features like speech recognition and energy sources.

Marketing: Fancy look-and-feel is the most important aspect for the remote control
Technological innovation is the second most important aspect
Target group is young and trendy
Speech recognition should be used for innovation
Buttons should be part of the remote control's style
Project Manager: The remote must look nice and be very basic with few buttons
Functions should include volume, channel selection, on-off, mute, and text TV
The remote should be flexible to withstand drops
Black and yellow are the required colors
Scroll wheels are cool for volume control
Industrial Designer: Received material possibilities to discuss
The case should be transparent for backlight to work
Considered kinetic or solar energy sources
LCD would require the most expensive chip
Buttons should be on top of the remote
User Interface: Speech recognition is recommended by research
The design should be modern with changeable fonts
Focus should be on lighting specific areas
Rubber case doesn't look good
Titanium was mentioned as a material option

agreements.details: The remote will include volume, channel selection, on-off, mute, and text TV buttons
The target demographic is young and trendy consumers
The case should be transparent for backlight functionality
Standard batteries will be used instead of rechargeable or kinetic options
Basic buttons should be on top of the remote
Flashy colors will be used for the remote
Speech recognition is a viable feature to include
Scroll wheels are acceptable for volume control

disagreements.details: Black and yellow colors don't go well together according to UI
Disagreement on whether channel buttons should be on the side or top
Rubber case is not preferred by UI
Concerns about the high cost of including an LCD display
Disagreement on whether the case should be flat or curved
Disagreement on whether scroll wheels should be used for volume
Disagreement on how to achieve technological innovation
Disagreement on which areas should be highlighted with backlighting

most_time_consuming.reason: The discussion around technological innovation, particularly speech recognition versus other features, consumed the most time as it involved debating feasibility, cost, and implementation options.
